text: Suppose we flip two fair coins and roll one fair six-sided die. What is the conditional probability that the number of heads equals the number showing on the die, conditional on knowing that the die showed 2?

steps: A = number of heads equals the number on the die

text: A website receives an average of 4 visitors every hour. a) What is the probability that up to 3 visitors are received during a 3-hour period? b) What is the probability that below 3 and 5 visitors (inclusive) are received in one hour?

text: Consider the following joint pdf for two random variables X and Y: f(x, y) = k * e^(-x-y), x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0 a) Find the constant k so that the PDF is normalized. b) Calculate the E[XY].

steps: Calculate E[XY] using the joint pdf
calculation: E[XY] = ∫∫ x * y * k * e^(-x-y) dx dy

text: Given the following data for three variables, X(hours of screen time per day), Y (hours of physical activity per day). a) Construct the covariance matrix of X and Y. b) Calculate the correlation coefficient.

steps: Calculate variance of X and Y

text: The number X of calls to a telephone exchange during the busiest hour is Poisson(λ). During five days the following observations on X were made: 125, 82, 87, 99, 92. Compute a 95% confidence interval for λ.

steps: Use Poisson distribution to find confidence interval

text: A certain drug is to be rated either effective or ineffective. Suppose lab results indicate that 75% of the time the drug increases the lifespan of a patient by 5 years (effective) and 25% of the time the drug causes a complication which decreases the lifespan of a patient by 1 year (ineffective). As part of a study you administer the drug to 10000 patients. a) Find the standard error for the sampling distribution. b) What is the probability that the lifespans of those in the study will be increased by an average of 3.55 years or below?

steps: Use normal distribution to find probability
